name: Hsiao, Hongwei
recent_1: Hsiao, H., Kau, T. Y., & Bradtmiller, B. (2024). A cluster-based law enforcement body armor sizing system: Concept, procedure, and design practice. Applied Ergonomics, 117, 104201. DOI: 10.1016/j.apergo.2023.104201
recent_1_synopsis: Using national anthropometric survey data on U.S. law enforcement officers, this paper develops a cluster-based sizing system to replace the inconsistent body-armor size structures (ranging from 6 to 25 sizes) found across the marketplace. It derives a rationalized nine-size system for male officers, grading vests by chest, waist, and torso length. The work matters because it provides a standardized, anthropometrically grounded basis for armor sizing that improves fit, coverage, and protection for a diverse officer population.
recent_2: Hsiao, H. (2024). Association of anthropometric characteristics of law enforcement officers with perceived ratings of fit, comfort, and pain in the use of body armor. Ergonomics, 67(4), 541-565. DOI: 10.1080/00140139.2023.2232581
recent_2_synopsis: Drawing on a national study of 974 law enforcement officers, this paper quantifies how officers' body dimensions relate to their perceived body-armor fit, discomfort, and armor-caused pain, and identifies the torso measurements most influential for sizing. It links subjective wear experience to specific anthropometric drivers. The findings inform armor sizing and design choices that can reduce discomfort and improve sustained use of protective equipment.
recent_3: Hsiao, H., Kau, T. Y., Whisler, R., & Zwiener, J. (2022). Body Models of Law Enforcement Officers for Cruiser Cab Accommodation Simulation. Human Factors, 66(5), 1350-1386. DOI: 10.1177/00187208221140220
recent_3_synopsis: This study builds multivariate body models of U.S. law enforcement officers (756 male and 218 female officers, with and without duty gear) from a stratified national survey to support digital simulation of how officers fit within police cruiser cabs. The models capture the size and shape variation, including encumbrance from gear, that must be accommodated in workspace design. It provides design-ready boundary cases for evaluating cruiser layouts, ingress/egress, and reach for the LEO population.
seminal_1: Hsiao, H., Chang, J., & Simeonov, P. (2018). Preventing Emergency Vehicle Crashes: Status and Challenges of Human Factors Issues. Human Factors, 60(7), 1048-1072. DOI: 10.1177/0018720818786132
seminal_1_synopsis: This review synthesizes the human factors challenges underlying emergency-vehicle crashes (ambulances, fire apparatus, police cruisers), spanning vehicle conspicuity, driver behavior, occupant restraint, cab design, and roadway/operational factors, and lays out research and intervention priorities. With approximately 76 citations (Crossref, retrieved 2026-06-17), it is among his most-cited works and is foundational for framing emergency-vehicle safety as a systems-level human factors problem that integrates anthropometry, vehicle design, and operator performance.
seminal_2: Hsiao, H. (2013). Anthropometric Procedures for Protective Equipment Sizing and Design. Human Factors, 55(1), 6-35. DOI: 10.1177/0018720812465640
seminal_2_synopsis: This article lays out four anthropometric design approaches (univariate, bivariate/probability-distribution, multivariate, and shape-based methods) and demonstrates them using large occupational datasets (tractor operators, respirator users, firefighters, and civilian workers) to guide sizing and design of protective equipment. With roughly 48-67 citations (Crossref ~48; scispace/Google Scholar ~67, retrieved 2026-06-17), it is foundational because it provides a coherent methodological framework that bridges anthropometric data and protective-equipment design decisions across occupational populations.
expertise_overview: Occupational anthropometry and its application to the design and sizing of personal protective equipment, vehicles, and workspaces for diverse worker populations. A central methodological thread is multivariate and shape-based anthropometric modeling, including 3D body scanning, digital human modeling, and statistical body-model construction to derive design boundary cases. Application domains span law enforcement officers, firefighters, truck drivers, and emergency responders, with work on body-armor sizing, fall-arrest harness design, respirator fit, vehicle cab accommodation, and protective-equipment fit, comfort, and pain. A complementary focus is fall prevention and protection, including suspension trauma and harness fit, and the broader human factors of emergency-vehicle safety. Methods include national stratified anthropometric surveys (often via mobile data-collection laboratories), cluster analysis for sizing systems, and simulation-based accommodation assessment. The overarching aim is to translate body size and shape variability into evidence-based design standards that reduce occupational injury.
notes: Identity confirmed with high confidence as Hongwei Hsiao of the National Institute for Occupational Safety and Health (NIOSH), Chief of the Protective Technology Branch and founder of the NIOSH Occupational Anthropometry Program/Laboratory. ORCID 0000-0002-2201-760X. Sources confirming identity: NIOSH/CDC anthropometry program pages (cdc.gov/niosh/anthropometry), the Samuel J. Heyman Service to America Medals honoree page (servicetoamericamedals.org/honorees/hongwei-hsiao), dblp (dblp.org/pid/59/7915.html), PubMed/PMC records, and SAGE/Human Factors and Applied Ergonomics author bylines, all matching the four pre-verified Human Factors hf_papers on law enforcement anthropometry. Disambiguation: distinguished from unrelated 'Hsiao' authors by the consistent NIOSH affiliation, anthropometry/protective-equipment/fall-protection topic cluster, and shared co-authors (Bradtmiller, Kau, Whisler, Zwiener). The three recent papers (2022-2024) are his latest substantive research articles; the Cruiser Cab paper carries a 2022 online/issued date in Crossref (and a 2024 print issue, vol 66(5)) and is retained as recent. Citation counts: Crossref is-referenced-by-count retrieved 2026-06-17 (Emergency Vehicle Crashes ~76; Anthropometric Procedures ~48); scispace/Google Scholar list ~66-67 for both, hence the approximate ranges; counts are approximate and may drift. All 5 DOIs cited in this profile were verified via the Crossref API (https://api.crossref.org/works/<DOI>) on 2026-06-17 and returned HTTP 200 with matching titles. No DOIs were invented and no unverifiable citations remain.
